undergoing field-testing to assure quality control.”
                                                                             and worked up through the ladder, tickling skin as the seconds
   “Excellent. The time comes soon when the Falcon will spread its
                                                                             ticked down. With little choice, Nikol set aside her annoyance.
wings to blood a new flock of warriors.” Khan Pryde turned away
to discuss something with saKhan Clees.                                      Anticipation spiked as she slid into the ProtoMech’s chest cavity,
    Though he knew his Khan considered his report delivered,                 kicking away the ladder, pulled down the skein of wires connect-
Joach nervously cleared his throat.                                          ing the neurohelmet and positioned it properly on her scalp. She
    Marthe looked back, cool eyes biting.                                    quickly stripped the padding from a row of small monitors to her
    Despite misgivings about some of the decisions made in design-           right as she ran a final course diagnostic. Then she activated the
ing the new ProtoMech (who was he to question), he finished his              remote control they’d rigged for the makeshift gantry that held
report. “The Erinyes will also be ready for combat by the end of the         her Roc’s chest plate. The machine trembled and finally heaved
year.”                                                                       toward her. Replacing the heavy padding over the monitors, she
    He repressed a desire to raise his arms at the rustling that swept       folded into a fetal-like position in the padded cavity as the chest
the room; an impression of a flock of raptors taking wing in angry           plate banged into place. Locking mechanisms clanged, while
surprise and zeroing in on the disturbance: Joach.                           ear drums popped with equalizing pressure. As the ProtoMech
    Khan Pryde’s gaze turned colder. “Then we shall see if the new           gained life, dozens of overlapping plates of the enclosure
trinket is worth Falcon effort, or if Clan Blood Spirit and those who        expertly adjusted to her size and weight until she eventually
follow their lead are as deluded as ever.”                                   floated as though in the synthetic amniotic fluid of a Trueborn
    Joach bowed and backed out of the room.                                  gestation chamber.
   Power surged to the neurohelmet, which synched with the
enhanced imaging circuitry along her shaved scalp. The first
waves of pleasure swept through and warmth blossomed in the
pit of her stomach. Three prongs snapped down from the back
edge of the neurohelmet, just piercing neck skin, completing
the circuit between the neurohelmet, her suit and the enhanced
imaging circuitry that ran the length of her exposed skin. The
pleasure surge broke in a torrent…
  …and the world ballooned and expanded. Dull existence fell
away, as though she stepped out of her body, beyond a transpar-
ent pane into another reality. She enlarged and expanded until
she stood a giant. Meters taller. Stronger. Solid, metal legs and
arms now her own; she knew her expression never changed, but
the true inner ferocity of her warrior’s visage was now etched in
steely glowing eyes and a sharp, hooked alloy beak: she perceived
her new metallic corporeal existence sheathing her conscious-
ness as she never could her frail flesh.
   Her blood flowed like magma, hot and demanding, her need
for more and more a hot knife twisting her gut. She swam in
raging ecstasy, her awareness expanded exponentially with
wracking pleasure/pain. As ever, the ecstasy moved in crescendo
as Nikol sensed another veil of existence behind her. And another
beyond that. And another. Levels of perfect integration and inter-
face. Levels of ecstasy and agony. Of understanding and percep-
tions. Mirrors within mirrors within mirrors; planes of knowledge
and existence marching off in an endless corridor, beckoning.
   In a small corner of her mind, Nikol recalled the pain she would
experience upon interface termination until she could inject
the drugs to keep her sanity in the dull existence outside of her
ProtoMech…knew at this moment it mattered not at all.
   The surging ecstasy finally gained equilibrium and her senses
focused down to a razor wire: her body stood rock solid, ready
to rend armor, to tear enemies—to become the ultimate warrior.
   Her mind willed her ProtoMech into motion. It instantly
responded, a hungry predator stalking toward a swift decapita-
tion that would sunder the Lyran defender’s head from its body
and cede the Falcons the world of Melissia.
   It is not too late. MechWarriors believe they are the ultimate
warriors in their twelve-meter tall giants. Yet they know nothing.
They scorn our half-sized ’Mechs, but they have no understanding
of what we bring to the strength of Falcon’s talons and beak. They
bring death. But I am become death.
   A God of War.

SCIENTIST CASTE TESTING STATION, LAST CHANCE
JADE FALCON OCCUPATION ZONE
15 MAY 3065

   Though she knew the building scrubbed the air of any partic-
ulates from the Torrence Iron Mines located a half-hundred kilo-
meters away, her nose twitched as her brain told Nikol she could
still smell it. The dust coated the exterior of the scientific outpost,
turning to clumpy, blackened streaks in the near-constant rains
of spring that reminded her of the congealed blood slicking the
burned remains of her protokin’s Erinyes. She distantly noted
her dirty fingertips rasping a nervous rat-a-tap-tap on the worn
vinyl of her chair’s arm rest, but couldn’t bring herself to chas-
tisement as her mind grasped at straws.

flowing across his computer terminal.                                     the Clan Council.”
  “Nikol?” Etienne Balzac responded, straightening from his own             “Yet I still do not understand. Why do we keep experimenting?
hunch over a monitor, rubbing a sore back. His ancient features           And how could you know she would cancel the program?” The
matched his infamous reputation. Despite their close work over            sinking feeling in the pit of his stomach opened like the fabric of
the last year and more, Joach’s resolve to question the ex-Scientist-     reality torn asunder during a hyperspace jump. He knew…had
General concerning Nikol and their work here died against the             known for years and refused to believe, until now. The horrid reality
emptiness of Etienne’s black-eyed stare.                                  burned, and yet what sliced through it all like a hot energy beam
   Why are we doing this? What are we doing? Why do we try to create      was the crystal-clear knowledge that even the monstrousness of
a superior ProtoMech and pilot if the Jade Falcon program has been        the act would not drive him away. Would not drive him from this
eliminated? The questions hammered relentlessly against his inabil-       bone that might eventually pull him out of this hell-hole. He’d sold
ity to confront Etienne. He cleared his throat. “The subject.”            his soul and everything with it to Etienne Balzac and his endless
   Etienne nodded. “Aff, the subject is adapting very well.”              machinations. I am sorry, Nikol….
   Silence descended except for the tapping of keys as two addi-            “Because I made sure of it.”
tional scientists in the control room continued diligent work. Joach
grew increasingly uncomfortable as Etienne continued to stare at
him. Warmth flushed his cheeks as a smile played at the corners of
the older man’s lips. “You have a question?”                                 Etienne enjoyed the stunned flush that swept Joach’s features.
   Joach started, surprised. In all the time they’d worked together,      The game of cat and mouse was almost more enjoyable at times
the man never asked him that. “A question?”                               than scientific research. It was art. The way he used and placed
  “Aff. You have a question.”                                             emphasis in his actions to highlight only what he wanted them to
   Joach licked his lips as he glanced away. Can I ask? He saw the        see. Making an object appear important to him, to hide the truth; a
ProtoMech, and for a moment the haunted look of a tortured                flat object made round. Chiaroscuro.
warrior overlaid everything before him. He took a deep breath and            You think I will hang myself, Kael, but I know you watch. And I hide my
spoke. “Why are we doing this? The program has been canceled.             actions in plain sight, right underneath your mechanically deformed nose.
Why do we continue to increase ProtoMech capabilities and to                 Joach stammered. “You manipulated the Khan into canceling the
adapt the pilots even more to their integration?” Why do we torment       ProtoMech program.”
Nikol? The final question hovered, unasked. Joach knew it would              Though it came from the younger man as an accusation, the
cross the line, despite Etienne’s apparent good mood.                     words flowed past Etienne without a ripple. “Of course.”
   The other man’s half-smile stayed in place as he smoothed wrin-          “And Renata Salk?”
kled hands across his bald pate. “Joach, I continue to work because         “An innocuous bureaucrat.” He waved his hand. “If the Khan wishes
my work is needed.”                                                       the Scientist-General not to be manipulated by outside forces, she
  “But the Scientist-General has agreed with the Khan,” Joach             should not fill such a vital role with a sycophant.”
responded. “They failed during the Falcon Incursion into House               Joach opened his mouth. Nothing came out for long seconds as
Steiner. And failure is discarded.”                                       the full impact of Etienne’s words settled in.
  “Of course it is.”                                                         You must learn to ask your questions, Joach. Questions are what
  “What?” A feeling in the depths percolated. A feeling he’d had for      make us human.
years, but could never articulate. Could never allow articulation.          “I fail to see…” Joach finally choked out.
  “Of course I knew the program would be canceled.”                         “You will.” Etienne crossed over to him, gripping his arm in an
  “You could not have expected it?!”                                      unusual display of physical contact. “When you piece the puzzle
  “Of course I did.”                                                      together, you will be ready. I am confident you will find it eventually.”

13 JANUARY 3071                                                                                                     patently obvious as usual.
                                                                                                                        More than two years
   With thunderous, delib-                                                                                          of occupation—t wo
erate strides, the Blue Flame                                                                                       years spent hiding from

ing through meter-thick                                                                                              BattleMech and fighter

The footfalls shook the earth despite the snow’s cushioning effect,                                            scavenge for food, parts, and
sending icicles raining down from the blackened skeletons of             maybe a little revenge along the way—had taught the survivors
nearby structures. Red-tinged cockpit glass lent the 45-ton quad-        of the Blakist Ragnarok that the invaders had eyes everywhere.
ruped an otherworldly look, like the eyes of a ghostly mechanical           Even when help came from outside, it had invariably failed. The
hellhound, searching the charred factory ruins for prey.                 Word was always one step ahead. All-knowing. All-powerful. They
   And all the while, its prey watched back.                             had kept an Archon imprisoned on his throne world, stymied
   Through the rangefinder on his targeting display, Sergeant            every effort to reclaim the Alliance, crushed the Lyran spirit with
Major Rolf Solomon watched the Blakist monster with a steady             victory after bloody victory.
gaze. His breath, tinged with the stink of stale coffee, puffed frosty      Conventional wisdom now held that they could not be stopped.
clouds in the cabin despite the woolen black scarf that covered             And Kramer was entirely too full of conventional wisdom right now.
most of his face. Even with the insulation built into the armored          “Halt die Klappe, Leutnant,” another voice hissed back, harsh and
hide of his Manteuffel attack tank—so new Rolf could still smell         stern, yet echoing softly within the Manteuffel and Solomon’s
the factory welds over his own stench—the effort to keep out the         earpiece. Hauptmann Stella Heigl occupied the tank’s command
bite of another Tharkan winter was relentless. Hiding beneath two        station directly behind him. Silent for hours, her sudden reaction
days’ worth of drifting snow, with only a Magna 350 Extralight—          to Kramer’s defeatism startled Solomon, but he did not dare flinch
set to low idle to avoid discovery—for heat…                             from the targeting display. His reticule settled over the Blue Flame’s
   Sometimes, playing dead came too close to the real thing.             stubby snout.
   The Flame slowed right in front of him, the armored beast sud-           They may know we’re here, Solomon told himself, but they don’t
denly hesitant about moving forward. Solomon frowned.                    know exactly where.
  “They know we’re here!” Though spoken barely louder than                 “Jäger One,” a new voice chimed in, barely above a mutter. This
a whisper, and carried over a hundred meters to his headset              one came from Sergeant Isaka, whose older Pegasus hid beneath
 a drift in the shadow of a ruined Buccaneer DropShip half a
 kilometer further east. “Forward shows a new contact inbound.
 Support-model Legacy, with black skull markings.”
     Solomon’s eyes narrowed.
     The Marauder of Mako Valley, at last…
   “That’s him,” Heigl replied. “Everyone get ready.”
     Solomon sucked in a breath and nodded imperceptibly as his
 fingers tensed on the trigger and the engines thrummed to life
 behind him.
     The Legacy appeared on Solomon’s tactical display as a simple
 red dot on the far right edge of the board, while the Blue Flame
 stood due north, barely a finger’s breadth away. Surrounding
 the Flame were six blue dots, the hidden members of half
 his company. Six armored hunters, hidden among the ruins,
 beneath snow and ice.
     The EISENJÄGER—Iron Hunters.
     Solomon grinned inwardly. Finally, Precentor Roman
 McKinnsey, the Marauder of Mako Valley, would be the
 Jäger’s prey.
     Suddenly, the Blue Flame shifted, kicking up snow as its body
 swung around. Its menacing glare turned straight toward the
 Manteuffel. Solomon released his breath with a hiss..
     Come back to tour your latest atrocity, monster? He thought
 bitterly, remembering the hard-fought battle here that pitted
 a hodgepodge of corporate security, mercenary troops and a
 remnant of LAAF forces against a wall of Blakist shock troops.
 The Jägers missed the fight, but all of Tharkad heard of the
 outcome, saw the mangled ruins of the factory complex, the
 smoking remains of DropShip hulls and the twisted, blackened
 hulks of BattleMechs and hastily converted WorkMechs—the
 pitiful wreckage that remained after the Marauder of Mako
 Valley called in another orbital weapons strike from the cap-
 tured WarShip above. Captives taken from the battle went to
 gods-only-knew-where, but Solomon remembered the Blakists’
 propaganda-vids showing lines of civvies and military prison-
 ers being dragged from the rubble and escorted at gunpoint by
’Mechs and battle-suit squads after McKinnsey’s forces mopped
 up the remaining defenders.
     Another “glorious” victory for the Word of Blake. Solomon had
 to force his jaw to unclench.
     Kramer’s voice cursed on the tactical channel. With no further
 warning, the earth around the Flame’s hindquarters exploded
 amid the roaring blasts of autocannon fire. One burst caught
 the Blakist ’Mech square on its right rear flank, gouging
 away armor and clawing at the SRM housings that
 rode just above the hip. Shaken by the loss
 of more than a ton of armor, but far too
 stable and skilled to fall down, the
 ghostly hound twisted toward
 Kramer’s Fortune wheeled
 assault tank—unseen
 f r o m S o l o m o n ’s
 vantage point—and
 unleashed a pair
 of angry sapphire
 laser bolts.

 channel as well. “Kramer, you Arschloch! ECM, now! EISENJÄGER           the entire field before his rangefinders while brushing away the
 open fire!”                                                             48-hour accumulation of snow and ice.

 had once been a Blue Flame.                                             plant became a brief stutter and the cabin lights flickered.

 action beyond this little intersection, and the news was not good:      pable of penetrating the armor with a single hit, the two bolts
    The Marauder’s Legacy had brought more friends.                      staggered the ’Mech just long enough for Solomon’s second

 two Thugs, in support of target Legacy! We have lost Forward            skull logo beyond recognition.
 Three and Four!”                                                          “Got you!” Solomon hissed.

 target. That bastard’s not getting away this time.”                     new smell, the oily stench of fused electronics, and a rattling
    Solomon nodded again, more to himself than to anyone. With           sound somewhere outside that sent a harsh vibration through
 Kramer’s panicked fire, they had lost the element of surprise,          the cabin. Had one of those missiles penetrated after all?
 and already, two hovertank crews—whose vehicles sacrificed                “It’s a drive wheel,” Corporal Reuben came back, his voice piped
 armor for speed—had paid the ultimate price. Now three assault          in from the rear section. “Left flank number three. We’re slipping,

 stood against a depleted company of vehicles.                             “Not too badly?” Jorg echoed in a voice laced with contempt.

 trio of medium lasers to his next volley. Even as the image of the            away from his flickering targeting displays.

 his eyes shut to clear his vision.                                               Solomon nodded and returned to his post, turning away and trying

 tant as his Fortune finally reached range and opened up with its                “Jorg!” he croaked after a moment, “bear left!”

 splash harmlessly in the battle-churned snow.                                 leg gimped and missing its right arm, the BattleMech was strug-
    Two flights of missiles showered the stricken machine next,                gling to get between its barely damaged partners—two Thugs

 the battlefield.                                                                 A heartbeat later, a quartet of PPC bolts washed over his crip-

    The PPCs flashed again, and this time Solomon felt the hits as a              At the end of the day, the
 thunderous pounding that shook the entire cabin. A fiery tingle               EISENJÄGER got their man.
 made his fingers spasm and set his skin crawling along the back of
 his neck. His vision blanked for a moment—he was sure it was only a
 moment—and popping sounds echoed around him. He opened his
 eyes to find sparks still dancing across the targeting panels, telltale
 signs of a hit that had penetrated armor to touch the deli-
 cate electronics.

                               In the Chaos March, a Tikonov Republican Guard mixed force deploys in the hopes of catching Word of Blake insurgents.

  BattleMechs reign supreme on the battlefield, but Combat                                   GROUND COMBAT VEHICLES
Vehicles can hold their own in battle. Though not as tough as                                   Ground Combat Vehicles use the standard rules for resolving
BattleMechs, they are generally much cheaper and easier to build.                            hit locations, with the following exceptions.
BattleTech provides rules for four types of vehicles: ground, VTOL,
WiGE and naval, as defined in Components (see p. 20).                                       Attack Directions
  Most of the rules regarding the use of Combat Vehicles in the                                The diagram shows the front,
game are contained in the Movement and Combat sections. Those                               side and rear attack directions
rules are not repeated here; instead, this section covers rules that                        for all Combat Vehicles.
only apply to specific Combat Vehicles, along with hit location
and critical hits tables used exclusively for Combat Vehicles.                              Determining Hit Location
                                                                                               Combat Vehicles take hits
                                                                                            to four or five locations: Front,

                                                                                            Turret or Rotor (if applicable).

make an immediate roll on the Motive System Damage Table; see                                  Armor-Piercing Special Munitions: Every successful attack

to-hit modifiers; a turret rotation is treated exactly like a ’Mech torso                      Turret Hits: If the Combat Vehicle has no turret, a turret hit

dard rules for firing arcs as well, with two exceptions: side-mounted

Ground Combat Vehicle Critical Hit Effects
   Critical hits only affect items in the location struck. If the
                                                                                                                                                                                    INDEX
item listed on the Ground Combat Vehicle Critical Hits Table
does not apply in that location (the item doesn’t exist, only one
such critical hit can occur per location and so on), simply move
down the column until an applicable critical result is achieved.
For example, a dice roll result of 9 for the Rear location indi-
cates Weapon Destroyed. If the vehicle mounts no weapons

hit, see below. The engine itself remains func-

for every 10 points (or fraction there of ) of
a weapon’s Damage Value, apply 1 point of

of damage each, while a heavy Gauss Rifle at                                   Yellow Jacket Gunboat, Tikonov Republican Guard (House Steiner)

                                                                                                                                                           199
                                                                                         Kevin Killiany




             The Twelfth Deneb Light Cavalry on David was attacked by the Ninth Dieron
           Regulars and Brion’s Legion. ... The early stages of the fight for David were
           inconclusive. The efforts of Combine forces to pin down the Deneb ’Mechs were
           thwarted repeatedly by a determined militia and the actions of civilians, who
           aided the AFFS forces whenever possible.


             —From Fourth Succession War Military Atlas, Volume 1, by Doctor B. Banzai
           (copyright March 3029), by permission of the New Avalon Institute of Science

OREGON PLAINS                                                               Ty’s untrained eye, but he knew it made Drac sense for the boss to
ANDROPO, DAVID                                                              drive a Drac ’Mech even if a better one was available.
DRACONIS MARCH, FEDERATED SUNS                                                 Coming up on the end of the pass, Ty could see he was running
17 APRIL 3029                                                               out of field. The Combine BattleMechs were standing between
                                                                            him and the access road. He’d either have to drive straight into
   Ty pretended he couldn’t see the Dragon standing at the edge of          one or admit he could see them.
the field as he finished his final pass. He kept his head turned, looking      Tough choice. At the last moment he snapped his head back,
back over one shoulder at the whirling disks of metal as they pulver-       dropping his jaw in amazement as he stared up at the towering
ized the ground cover, shredding the thick sod and driving it down into     war machines.
the topsoil. The ground and vegetation were too moist for a dust cloud.        Yes, folks, the local yokel is an idiot.
   He glanced forward again, keeping his head low. He hoped the                The driver of the Dragon said something over his external
hat brim shielding his eyes from the reddish sun would give the             speakers. Maybe her external speakers. Ty couldn’t tell for sure
impression he was only checking his ancient tractor’s alignment             over the yammer of his tractor’s aging diesel. He made a show of
with previous swaths.                                                       cupping his ear and gaping while gunning the engine just a bit.
   A second BattleMech had come out of the sandalwoods to                      He’d laid it on too thick. The Drac ’Mech jockey let him know
stand behind the other’s right shoulder. It was bigger than the             he—or she—wasn’t buying it by pointing the Dragon’s left arm at
Dragon. Given the Dracs’ passion for appearances, Ty would have             the tractor’s nose.
expected the bigger boss to drive the bigger machine, but its po-              Not anxious to see what a medium laser could do to a six-hundred-
sition indicated the newcomer was of lower rank.                            year old Deere, Ty—with much comic fumbling—shut his machine
   As if rank mattered when two BattleMechs confronted a simple             down. Any way he could waste these guys’ time was a victory.
farmer driving his tractor.                                                   “This is the wrong time of year to harrow,” the MechWarrior’s
   Ty decided the big guy wasn’t a Drac design. It had hulking              voice boomed in the sudden silence. Definitely a woman. “This
rotator shoulders like the Dragon, but its cockpit was lower and            field should be left fallow.”
thrust forward, making it resemble a lowlife brawler more than                 A hundred Drac ‘Mech jockeys on David, and Ty had to draw the
the noble warrior look the Dracs preferred. It looked tougher to            only farmer.
   “Killing dung voles, Captain,” he called back, then kicked
 himself for revealing that he’d recognized the blue katana “five”
 badge on the Dragon’s breast. He could also read the unit desig-
 nations—four weeks of resistance had taught him a little about
 the invaders. Commander of Second Company, First Battalion
 of the Ninth Dieron Regulars would be—Ty flipped through his
 mental files—Tai-i Lisa Montgomery.
   “Trying to, anyway,” he went on, hoping she hadn’t noticed the
 pause during his mental gymnastics. “Once they get in a field like
 this, the only way to keep them from spreading to the crops is burn
‘em out or chop ‘em up. They don’t dig deep,” he added helpfully.
    The Dragon kept its medium laser leveled at his tractor. Ty sus-
 pected the first indication he’d have that Montgomery knew no
 dung voles existed would be a bolt of coherent light burning
 him to smoke.
   “Did a group of BattleMechs pass this way?”
    You mean the Twelfth Deneb’s recon lance? Ty thought. I was just
 covering their tracks.
   “No, ma’am,” he said. “Until you two popped up, I hadn’t seen
 a soul.”
    On the heels of his words, two more Combine BattleMechs
 stepped out of the forest—both good, solid, predictably Drac-
 built Lancelots.
    Ty felt his mouth go dry as he realized one had come from
 the north. He waited out the silence as Second Company’s First
 Lance chose not to include him in their deliberations.
   “What is the metal building to the north?” Montgomery asked.
 Ty wished she’d point her laser elsewhere.
   “That’d be my tractor shed,” he said. No point in playing too dumb,
 particularly with the tai-i clearly not buying his innocence. “It’s full
 of tractors, harvesters, stuff like that. Half of it doesn’t work.” Then,
 making no effort to hide his pride: “The Jones farm has been here
 ever since folks colonized David.”
   “Jones farm?”
   “What you’re standing on,” Ty said, then swept his arms to illus-
 trate. “From Mount Shasta to the Sometimey River; from Taylor east
 to the desert. Grains, vegetables, koi ponds and timber. That’s Jones
 farm and I’m Tybalt Jones.”
   “The owner of such an estate plows the
 fields like a common hand?”
   “The owner of such a working farm.”
 Ty had no trouble putting an appro-
 priate level of pride-fueled heat into
 his words. “I spend twelve hours a
 day, seven days a week, keeping ev-
 erything running right.”
    Pause. Unmoving laser.
   “Show us this tractor shed.”
    The shed—which Ty had to admit was
 actually an extensive garage that covered
 a good quarter of an acre—stood at the north
 end of Home Ridge, a long hill named by the third-
 generation Jones. Ty didn’t imagine his long-dead
 ancestor had put much thought into naming
 the place after building the family home-
 stead on its highest point.
    Building high was a David tradition
 without explanation, as far as Ty knew. On
 such an arid world, everyone built their
houses as high above the plains as they could, as though expect-          There had not been enough vaccine—or enough time—for
ing floods momentarily.                                                the FedSuns MechWarriors and troops to have been immunized
   The badlands began just north of Home Ridge, the abrupt             against David’s native microorganisms. Though the water could
transition to rocky hardpan demarking the end of the Jones fam-        be boiled and irradiated to purity, the Twelfth Deneb had been
ily’s terraforming efforts. The ground was not stone, but even the     forced to live on hoarded battle rations, dependent on local sup-
Drac assault ‘Mech failed to mark the packed clay with its passing.    porters for fresh supplies.
   The treeless land was rumpled like a coarse blanket thrown             One man could not work fast enough, and Ty found himself
on the ground, a place of blind arroyos and abrupt escarpments.        politely moved aside as a squad of volunteers took over food
The broad shape of Danson’s Hill was visible to the northeast and      distribution. Stepping back, he looked up at the narrow sliver of
the rugged white cone of Mount Shasta dominated the northern           grey sky visible past the overhanging roof of the shallow cave—
horizon. Ty made certain not to glance toward the peak, crowned        more an alcove, he thought—that shielded Second Battalion
with white mineral deposits that gave the illusion of year-round       from sight of any Drac overflights.
snow—and that reflected most active sensors.                             “Any news, Tybalt?”
   He made a production of working the clumsy mechanical                  Ty turned to find Major Marcia Teon at his elbow. Almost liter-
combination lock that secured the shed’s double doors. They            ally. At maybe fifty kilos and a hundred and sixty centimeters, she
should not have been locked at midday. In fact, they had been          was the smallest MechWarrior he’d ever seen.
wide open, giving Sid and the boys light and air while they’d             She smiled at his startled reaction, her chocolate-brown
stripped a harvester when Ty had taken the harrow out. No              cheeks squeezing her grey-green eyes nearly shut. Ty suspected
doubt his mechanic and farm hands had run for the woods when           that if she ever gave up the military’s strict physical training she’d
they’d got wind of the Dracs.                                          turn plump. The thought of a butterball MechWarrior made him
   The doors were high enough and wide enough for the Dragon           smile in return.
to enter. Montgomery didn’t wait for the sodium lights far over-          Then he sobered as he remembered his news.
head to heat up. Instead, she turned on external spotlights,             “The Dracs have figured out that if they can’t find you any-
probing the shadows as she moved through the cavernous shed.           where, you must be somewhere their sensors don’t work,” he said.
One of the Lancelots filled the doorway, the muzzles of its two       “They’re eliminating the Badlands one area at a time.”
large lasers casting about in a vague search for targets.                “Any chance they’re saving us for last?”
   Ty would have stood back and waited for something to flush             Ty shook his head.
while the guys getting paid for it beat the underbrush, but the          “The Brion mercenaries are a few days east of here, headed this
Dracs worked backward. Or maybe it was a military thing. He            way,” he said. “The Ninth Dieron are farther south—they went
suspected their sensors had already told them there were no            after the flats south of the Sometimey first—but it’s a sure bet
BattleMechs present, but from the way the tai-i was examining          they’ll get here pronto once the mercs call for help.”
every truck and fruit gondola, there were probably enough simi-           Teon nodded. Her eyes scanned the shallow cave, one of
larities between farm equipment and military vehicles to make          several in the flanks of Mount Shasta that concealed elements of
eyes-on inspection necessary.                                          the Twelfth Deneb.
   From the way she was peering at everything, Ty guessed the             Ty had no idea what her orders were, but he couldn’t imagine
Drac captain wasn’t as familiar with tractors as she was with          this mountain as a good place for a last stand. The narrow gorges
growing seasons. He felt his pulse quicken as she spent several        and shallow caverns might favor the Deneb’s light ’Mechs, but
long seconds considering a rounded cylinder—the size of a ten-         they were easily blocked. A relatively small portion of the
thousand-liter water tank—protruding from a pile of parts and          Combine forces could keep the Federated Suns warriors from
vehicle panels.                                                        breaking free while the rest of them methodically blasted the
  “This is built for underwater,” Montgomery said, her amplified       mountain to rubble around them.
voice echoing off the corrugated metal walls.                             Or not. Just contain them until their food supply ran out and
  “It’s one of the oldest things in here,” Ty shouted back. “Maybe     they either surrendered or starved to death.
they needed to be sealed while terraforming?”                             Teon didn’t share her thoughts with Ty, of course. But it didn’t
   He got no answer. No doubt more convinced by the encum-             take a genius to guess the Twelfth’s job was to stay alive and
bering mound of dust-covered debris than Ty’s words, the Drac          keep the Dracs tied up until some kind of reinforcements got
captain moved away from his multiple-great grandfather’s most          here. The staying alive part meant Teon’s boss needed to get his
beloved treasure to examine a combine.                                 people out of this hidey-hole before they got trapped.
   Ty didn’t draw an easy breath until he saw the backs of the four       With the ocean to the north and most of Andropo’s civilian
BattleMechs leaving his land, heading south.                           population to the west, east and south were their only options.

MOUNT SHASTA, ANDROPO, DAVID                                           Dracs or through the mercs—both units as big as the Twelfth
DRACONIS MARCH, FEDERATED SUNS                                         used to be and both made up of heavy and assault ’Mechs.
22 APRIL 3029                                                            “We need intel,” Teon said, then looked quickly at Ty as though
                                                                       startled she’d spoken. “Sorry. You guys are doing one hell of a job.
 “Thank God.”                                                          But getting news a day or two old isn’t the same as hard numbers
  The captain made no effort to hide her delight as Ty threw           real time.”
back the tarp, revealing the crates of Combine MREs.                      It was Ty’s turn to look startled. But he only nodded as an idea
  Ty grinned, bending to lift the first case out of the trailer.       formed in the back of his mind.
OREGON PLAINS, ANDROPO, DAVID                                                 company commander. He guessed the habit of having the head

07 MAY 3029                                                                     “Name and business,” the mercenary captain demanded over his
                                                                              loudspeaker.
  “Look alive,” Ty called down into the control cabin. “We got four             “Ty Jones, farmer,” he shouted cheerfully, fighting the urge to
coming out of the woods up ahead.”                                            duck down inside the explorer. “Just taking the harvesters and
  “We got eight more in the woods,” Sid answered. “It’s spooky. I             processor over to the Lattimer place.”
can give you temperature, range, speed—I don’t know what all                     Sarah and Jenny, each perched atop a fruit picker and dressed to
these numbers mean. I think that’s the pilot’s shoe size.”                    risk sunburn and wind chill, smiled and waved at the BattleMech
   Ty pulled his head down into the cabin, still gripping the rim             on cue. Ty knew he’d taken a risk betting on male ‘Mech jockeys,
of what Sid called the skylight with both hands. His eyes couldn’t            but the odds were in his favor. Dressed as they were, he was
adjust to the dim interior after the noon glare, but he could see             betting his niece and Sid’s wife were pretty enough to keep the
the glow of a dozen screens.                                                  merc from looking too closely at the “processor.”
  “Shut those things down,” he said. “We don’t know how well                    “Stick to the main roads,” the merc’s voice grated. “When you get
those guys can see us.”                                                       where you’re going, stay put until you’re told you can travel.”
   He heard a series of slaps and clicks, and the screens went dark.            “The way Marge cooks, that’ll be a pleasure,” Ty said as though that
The only light was the smoky grey glow from the heavily polar-                were the best news ever. He waved to the departing BattleMechs.
ized windscreen. The kid driving peered straight ahead through                   A trailer with a conveyor leading into the rear cargo bay wasn’t
the thick ferroglass, oblivious to the activity behind him. Billy was         much of a disguise. But it was enough to fool folks who couldn’t
driving because that job required the least imagination.                      tell a Cortez explorer from a farm truck.
   Ty pulled his head back up into the sunlight and tried to make
balancing at the top of a truncated ladder look like relaxing in the
catbird seat. According to the schematics loaded into the comput-
er, he should have been head and shoulders inside a turret mount-                “Jones, how did you get these readings?”
ing a small laser.                                                               “Colonel, my great-great-gran laid some old tech away for a
   The turret, the laser and half the waldos built into the hide of            rainy day,” Ty grinned at the radio speaker as though the Twelfth
the ancient Cortez explorer had disappeared over the past half-                Deneb’s commander could see him.
millennium. Ty had no clue where to look for them and he didn’t                   Outside the skylight was as dark as the Cortez’s interior. After a
care. All that mattered was the Beagle Active Probe, which was in              day of casting back and forth in front of the mercenaries’ position,
place and working—just enough—after Ty and Sid took apart the                  he’d sent everything the Beagle Active Probe had recorded over the
pieces they didn’t recognize, cleaned them all carefully and put               off frequency the resistance used to communicate with the Twelfth.
them back exactly the way they’d found them.                                     “Don’t suppose you tagged Brion?” the colonel asked. “Chop the
   Ty raised his hand, ordering his little caravan to stop. No use pretend-    head off a personal merc group like this and they usually fall apart.”
ing he didn’t see the quartet of heavy ’Mechs striding toward them.              “No, sir,” Ty admitted.
   He didn’t need to see the bear paw badge to know these weren’t                 Figuring out that the equipment worked had been the extent of
Dracs. These ‘Mechs hailed from Brion’s Legion, the merc unit he’d             their success. They hadn’t a clue how to analyze the data they’d re-
heard about but never seen. The Twelfth had moved up to Danson’s               corded. He should have rolled his Cortez right up into the middle
Hill, a better place to make a stand and from which to make a quick            of the mercs, and then head honcho Brion would have stepped
break. From the way the mercs had been moving in these last few                forward to look them over himself—
days, they had a pretty good idea where the Twelfth was.                          Ty straightened abruptly, hitting his head on an unnamed box
   He hoped Brion didn’t field any farmers—or historians—among                 of electrics.
his MechWarriors.                                                                “Sid,” he said, holding his head and wondering if he felt blood.
   One ’Mech—a high-shouldered fireplug Ty thought might be                   “The soil analysis augers work?”
a JagerMech—stepped ahead of the others and leveled its arm-                     “We can dig holes and throw rocks with the best of them,” Sid
mounted cannon at his head.                                                    assured him. “What do we need with soil samples?”
   Ty could see from the markings that he’d drawn another captain.                Keying open the mike so the colonel could hear him as well, Ty
With a total of twelve ‘Mechs around, that meant this was another              laid out his plan.

           As [Brion’s Legion] advanced up a gentle slope toward the Deneb Light Cavalry, a Legion MechWarrior
        noticed that the ground ahead had been dug up and strewn about. Suspecting vibrabombs, Colonel
        Pythonius Brion’s Dragon moved to examine the freshly dug ground more closely. Finding no mines,
        an irritated Colonel Brion gave the order to advance. The Davion trick had succeeded, however, for
        Colonel Brion had identified himself to the watching Twelfth Deneb by moving to investigate the sup-
        posed minefield. An entire battalion trained its weapons on the commander as Brion’s Legion moved
        within range. In a moment, Colonel Brion’s ’Mech exploded, leaving only its legs standing on the
        field. The sudden loss of their commander demoralized the warriors of Brion’s Legion. When they
        broke and retreated, the ’Mechs of the Deneb Light Cavalry were in hot pursuit.

from Support Vehicles.
   Unless specifically noted otherwise, units use the same                                                                                                   SUPPORT
rules to mount and dismount from Support Vehicles as for                                                                                                     VEHICLES

(see Carrying Units, p. 89, in the Aerospace Movement section).

   Infantry: Use the Infantry Carriers rules (see p. 223) for                                                                                                AEROSPACE
infantry mounting and dismounting from Support Vehicles.                                                                                                       UNITS


MOUNTING                                                                                                                                                      CREATING
   To mount a Support Vehicle during a turn, a unit must start                                                                                               SCENARIOS
its Ground Movement Phase in the same hex as the carrier
(or an adjacent hex for Large Support Vehicles; the adjacent
                                                                                                                                                              PAINTING
hex must be at the same elevation as the Support Vehicle’s                                                                                                   MINIATURES
underlying hex). The unit may mount only at the end of the
carrier’s movement. Carrying units suffer no reductions in MP
for hauling other units as long as those units’ weight does not           RARA                                                                                INDEX
exceed the carrier’s own weight or cargo capacity (see Cargo
Carriers, p. 261).                                                              Mobile Army Surgical Hospital (MASH), Third Crucis Lancers (House Davion)

                BERKDALE, BRIGHTON                      Brighton sun. He tried to ignore his discomfort
               XIN SHENG COMMONALITY                    and absorb Siu’s instruction while more vehicles
             CAPELLAN CONFEDERATION                     passed through. The drivers waved their identifica-
            13 SEPTEMBER 3060                           tion cards at the reader while the scanners gave
                                                        their vehicles a thorough look. Some of them
            The screen showed translucent image         looked bored, some nervous, some strained to
        layered over translucent image. Determining     look casual. None of them looked happy. Lawson
      where one item ended and another began            couldn’t blame them.
    was not always easy, and occasionally Si-ben-bing      Fifteen more minutes went by. Then Siu abruptly
 Siu pressed a button on his terminal that told the     leaned forward and pressed a black button on his
vehicle outside to go even slower. He leaned forward    terminal. The checkpoint outside filled with flash-
and explained to Shia-ben-bing Lawson just what he      ing red lights, and an automated voice—a pleasant
was looking for.                                        but firm female tone—came on.
  “If you mean to do this job properly, the first         “Please pull over to the green square on your right.
thing you must remember is that many people are         Please pull over to the green square on your right.
coming down from the mountain mines, and can            Please pull over to the green square on your right.”
therefore be expected to have many of the tools            The voice did not mention what would happen if
you’d expect to see in such an operation. Do not        the vehicle didn’t comply, but by this point it didn’t
mistake those tools for any sort of weapon.             need to. The residents of the St. Ives Compact (or,
  “At the same time, be aware that potential insur-     as Chancellor Sun-Tzu Liao had renamed it, the
gents know mining equipment is regularly coming         Xin Sheng Commonality) were by this point quite
through the city’s checkpoints, and they seek to        familiar with the lengths to which the soldiers of
smuggle in weapons by disguising them as mining         the Confederation would go to enforce law and
equipment. Pay particular attention to such items.”     order. The car pulled into the square.
   So you’re telling me something that looks like          Siu stood. “Shia-ben-bing, come with me. You’ll
mining equipment may or may not be mining equip-        need to learn how to work this side of the opera-
ment, and that I should ignore it while simultaneous-   tion as well.”
ly paying particular attention to it, Lawson thought.      Lawson obediently followed Siu outside.
Part of him wanted to say the words aloud, but as         “Keep your hand near but not on your weapon,”
Siu’s trainee he didn’t dare.                           Siu instructed as they walked. “You don’t want to
   Lawson sat in the small yellow kiosk, perched on     make the subjects nervous, but you also don’t
an unpadded metal stool, surrounded by screens          want them to be able to shoot faster than you.”
and weapons and buttons. His uniform, with its            “Yes, sir,” Lawson said. Maybe someday Siu will give
long Liao-green sleeves, felt too warm for the          me a completely unambiguous piece of advice.
   The car in the green square was small and blue, a four-
seater. A woman sat behind the steering wheel, a man in the
passenger seat to her left. Siu walked up to the woman.
  “Ngh on,” he said politely, his long face dour. “May I ask your
business in Berkdale today?”
  “We live here,” the woman said shortly. Her black hair was
chin length, plastered to her head like a helmet, the front
ends curling in toward her mouth.
  “Then what took you out of the city?”
  “Business.”
  “I’ll need you to be more specific.”
  “A meeting with potential clients in Verdure. We run a cater-
ing business. We were discussing the terms of a job.”
  “I see. What sort of job?”
  “I don’t see how …”
  “I’m not asking you to see how it matters,” Siu said, his voice
level but firm. “I’m asking you what sort of a job it was.”
  “A wedding reception.”
  “At a private residence?”
  “Yes.”
  “Family of the bride or groom?”
  “Friend. Of the bride’s family.”
  “Will it be held indoors or outdoors?”
  “Now, this is too …”
  “Indoors or outdoors?”
  “Outdoors. The wedding is in October. The weather should
warm up nicely.”
  “What do you plan to serve?”
  “Pastries, cakes, cookies. Assorted sweets.”
  “The groom likes sweets?” Lawson noticed that Siu was
talking faster and faster.
  “The bride. These are friends of the bride.”
  “Of course. And how many people are they going to be
expecting?”
  “About two hundred.”
  “And their living room is large enough to hold all
those people?”
  “Yard. Their yard is big enough. The reception is
outdoors.”
  “Ah, yes, I believe you said that. Well, the yards
in Corson Creek are usually quite large.”
  “Verdure,” the woman said, her right hand
drumming a rapid rhythm on the door of
her car. “We were in Verdure.”
  “Fine. You may proceed.”
   The woman scowled at Siu as he
walked away. She looked ready to fly
away from the checkpoint with a
scream of burning rubber. Instead,
she pulled smoothly out of the
green box and drove off.
   Siu began offering more point-
ers as soon as the woman’s car
started moving. “Never apologize
for pulling someone over. Never
thank them for their time. They
should never feel like they are doing
you a favor. Ask them enough questions
to make them repeat their facts a couple of
times, but don’t rely on that. Any insurgents with even a small           “It’s just … not the best thing to say,” Lin said. “They’ve got a part in
degree of skill will have a good cover story worked out that you        this struggle just like we do. We shouldn’t treat them like an enemy.”
can’t easily shred. You need to use the interrogation time to look        “Okay, fine. Sorry,” Lawson said. “I guess it’s our part in the
for visual clues as well as listening to their responses.”              struggle that really bothers me. More than anything they do.”
  “What kind of clues?”                                                   “We’re infantry,” Lin said. “We do the things the big machines
   Siu, true to form, responded with contradictions as he walked        can’t. Sometimes that’s house-to-house combat. Sometimes
back toward their kiosk. “Be cautious of anyone who does not            open field operations. And sometimes—” she shrugged “—it’s
display any items that reflect the spirit of Xin Sheng. All Capellan    sitting in a kiosk helping keep a planet under control.” She smiled.
citizens should take part in this rebirth, especially those citizens   “Couldn’t really put a Ti Ts’ang on the roadside, could we?”
of the newly christened Xin Sheng Commonality. Resisting this             “I dunno,” Lawson said. “Could have a good effect on traveler
movement could be a sign of someone who harbors dangerous               morale. Good for us, I mean. Just seeing it hovering over the
thoughts. However, you must also beware of those who cover              highway would keep people in line.”
themselves in Xin Sheng items. Such a rapid assimilation of the           “But it would put them on their guard,” Lin countered.
ideals of their conquerors is suspicious and is likely insincere.      “Everyone would clam up on us. Bad for business.”
People who seem overly dedicated to the rebirth could be con-              Lawson gave a dissatisfied snort. But there wasn’t much to
cealing treasonous tendencies.”                                         be gained by complaining anymore. At least, not out loud. He
   Lawson dared venture a question. “Is there a specific number         kept a running internal monologue going, most of it complaints.
of Xin Sheng-related items that would be considered neither too         All the while, he imagined himself sitting by the checkpoint in
many nor too few?”                                                      the cockpit of a Ti Ts’ang, blasting anyone who didn’t follow his
   He tried to make his tone as innocent as possible. It wasn’t in-     instructions to smithereens.
nocent enough. Siu stopped and pivoted, hands on hips, glaring
at Lawson.
  “Impertinence is not accepted in any branch of the Chancellor’s
gwan deuih,” Siu snapped. “Perform your duty with a proper de-             Ten more days passed before Lawson got to lead his first ques-
meanor or you will not be in the service for long.”                     tioning. Siu didn’t like his trainees to think of it as interrogation,
   Lawson knew better than to attempt a response.                       as he believed that made them come off too heavy-handed. The
                                                                        driver, identified by his papers as Roderick Hirt, was a square-
                                                                        headed man with a short, flat haircut. He looked ready to rip
                                                                        Lawson in two. Lawson stood 1.7 meters, and he guessed the
   The rest of the day did nothing more to assuage his growing          driver was 1.9 and about 120 kilos. If the encounter turned physi-
dissatisfaction. He managed to contain his frustration until            cal, it could get bad—except, of course, for the fact that Lawson
the next morning, when Siu wandered to another kiosk in the             had his rifle.
checkpoint to enlighten another recruit with his unique wisdom.           “Ngh on, sir. Where are you coming in from?” He cringed as
Lawson was left with Shia-ben-bing Lin, and Shia-ben-bing Lin           soon as he delivered the sentence, waiting for Siu to correct his
was left to listen to Lawson’s rants.                                   poor grammar. His superior said nothing.
   He covered his irritation with Siu, his dislike of the glares he       “Been out of the city for a few days.”
regularly got from the citizens of Brighton, then moved on to his         “Yes, sir.” The computers at the checkpoint had scanned the car,
annoyances with his general assignment.                                 an Avanti Starfire that wore its newness with pride, as soon as it
  “Is this what you thought you were signing up for? Is this            came through. They had alerted Lawson that the car had been
what you thought you’d be doing?” he asked—though, as he                out of the city for exactly five days. “And where were you?”
hadn’t given Lin a chance to speak in the past twenty minutes,            “Different places. St. Simon, Paulson, Friedlander.”
the questions were purely rhetorical. “There’s a major offensive           All mining towns, Lawson thought—like pretty much every
going on all around us, in systems just a jump away, and we’re          small town in the area around Berkdale. “And what brought you
sitting here in a glorified tollbooth. We were trained to destroy,      out to those towns?”
and they’ve got us acting like bloody thought police—like we’re           “Business.”
the tian sha de Maskirovka or something.”                                  Unwilled, one of Siu’s “guidelines” came to Lawson’s mind. “Be
   Lin’s eyes widened. “Watch what you say!”                            suspicious of people who provide a bare minimum of information
  “Oh, who cares? You’re not Maskirovka, are you? Of course             in reply to your questions. It often means they have something to
not. And I’m not. So I can call them tian sha de if I want to. They     hide. At the same time, remember to respect the privacy of loyal
wouldn’t put me in irons just for a simple epithet, would they?”        citizens of the Confederation. If you encounter people who wish
   Both of them knew the answer to that question was likely “Yes,”      to withhold information from you in order to preserve their own
but neither of them said so.                                            dignity, you owe them a certain amount of respect. Though that
   He hadn’t succeeded in calming Lin. Her eyes darted back and         respect should never compromise your pursuit of the truth.”
forth; her right hand scratched her thigh. He knew she wasn’t just         Lawson tried to push all thoughts of Siu out of his mind.
nervous for his sake. For all she knew, he could be Maskirovka,           “What sort of business?”
and his little utterance could be an on-the-spot loyalty test. If         “Research. I’m with a commodities brokerage. Never hurts to see
she didn’t adequately rise to the Maskirovka’s defense, she might       how the actual mining and production of commodities is going.”
be the one in irons before the end of the day.                            “Looking into the silver production in Paulson?”
                                                                          “No silver in Paulson. Big copper mine there.”
   “Yes, yes, of course. Which brokerage firm did you say you               Siu’s expression didn’t change, but when he spoke, the conde-
 work for?”                                                              scension in his voice was gone.
   “I didn’t.”                                                             “I’m certain it was a good shooting,” he said.
   “Then please do.”
   “Waterman and Clask.”
   “On Fifteenth Street?”
   “Ninth.”                                                                 The incident made Lawson’s life better and worse. As it turned
   “I assume you slept in hotels on your trip?”                          out, Roderick Hirt really did work for the wealthy brokerage firm
   “Of course.”                                                          of Waterman and Clask; in fact, he was a well-regarded vice-pres-
   “Which ones?”                                                         ident. In the wake of his death, rumors about why he had been
   “I’m pretty sure I don’t need to tell you that.”                      killed, what he might have been involved in and what, by exten-
   “Actually, sir, you do. The names of the hotels, please.”             sion, his firm might have been involved in spread across the city.
    Hirt squinted. “The Accord, the Yu Yang, and the Deep Valley Inn.”   Wealthy clients, understandably nervous about having any of
   “That’s three.”                                                       their money attached to a firm with a trace of disloyalty, pulled
   “Right.”                                                              their money by the millions. Almost overnight, Waterman and
   “But you were gone five days. Four nights.”                           Clask’s business dried up. One of the most powerful brokerages
   “That’s not true. I was gone for three nights.”                       in the city became little more than a shell that had investments
   “Not according to our records.”                                       from Mr. Waterman, Mr. Clask and pretty much no one else.
   “Then your records are wrong.”                                           The whole thing, Lawson thought, made an effective dem-
   “I’m afraid that’s not possible.”                                     onstration. He regretted that he had to reveal his true position
   “It is.”                                                              to Siu, but his superiors had authorized the move. Not only had
   “Sir, you left Berkdale four nights ago. I need to know where         Lawson shut down Waterman and Clask’s financing pipeline
 you spent that fourth night.”                                           to St. Ives loyalists, but he’d effectively controlled Siu. The sup-
   “There was no fourth night!”                                          posed superior officer was now quite deferential to his trainee,
    Lawson put both hands on his automatic rifle. “Sir, I have to        and he no longer offered a hint of his “wisdom.” Lawson had long
 ask you to step out of your car.”                                       ago learned questioning techniques Siu had never even heard of,
   “This is ridiculous!”                                                 so Siu’s new silence on the subject was welcome.
   “Step out of the car!”                                                   It didn’t matter, though. He likely wouldn’t stay at the
   “All right, all right.”                                               checkpoint for long. He had shown the Maskirovka his worth in
    Hirt reached for the door handle. Then the Starfire’s engine         carrying out the assignment quickly and cleanly. Everything he
 gunned, its tires squealed, and the sporty red car shot off toward      planned—the computer tampering that made it look like Hirt
 Berkdale.                                                               had been gone for four days instead of three, the gizmo in the car
    Alarms blared across the checkpoint. Red lights flashed.             that locked the doors and made the vehicle accelerate away when
 Monocycle engines roared to life, ready to give pursuit.                Hirt was getting ready to exit—had worked to perfection. Lawson
    Lawson leveled his rifle and fired several rounds into the           didn’t even have to pull any strings to get the shooting cleared.
 back of the red car. Clear shots at someone who was clearly                He’d get into the battlefield soon. The front lines,
 an enemy.                                                               the heart of the ongoing battle. He was sure of it. He
    The first rounds bounced off the back window, but Lawson             could fight both sides then—using his Maskirovka
 wasn’t the only one firing at the fleeing vehicle, and eventu-          skills to weed out disloyalty in the Confederation
 ally the rounds had an impact. Glass flew away in chips, then           while aiming his weapon squarely at the faces of
 in chunks, then the entire back window fell inward like hail.           foreign foes. When he wasn’t doing the agency’s
 The rounds kept coming, and some of them found the driver.              dirty work, he’d be firing rounds at the enemy.
 The Starfire skidded off the road before it had traveled three          He knew well the importance of enforcing
 hundred meters.                                                         loyalty—what Maskirovka agent didn’t?—
    Siu, who had been firing right along with Lawson, turned to          but he’d recently gained an appreciation
 his recruit once the red car came to a complete stop.                   for the simple clarity of shooting people
   “It’s unfortunate that your interrogation came to this,” Siu said.    whose very uniform told you they were
“Perhaps you could have handled the situation in other ways,             your enemy. He’d spent years developing his
 ways we will discuss in the near future. But I believe you’ll be        own rules for intelligence work, rules that made
 able to clear this as a good shooting.”                                 Siu’s paradoxes seem cut-and-dried by comparison.
    Lawson kept his head tilted down, looking at his hands. It           He would, of course, keep following those rules, but
 might make him look downcast, which he wasn’t, but it also              when he needed to get away from them for a time,
 might make Siu follow his gaze downward.                                when he needed a black-and-white world, the
    It worked. Siu looked curiously at his recruit for a moment,         battlefield would be waiting for him. Dividing his
 then glanced down at Lawson’s hands. Then he saw something              time between enforcing loyalty to the Chancellor
 Lawson thought he needed to see.                                        and smiting the enemies of the Confederation—
                                                                         could any agent ask for more proper work?

   BattleMechs and vehicles are expensive to manufacture                                    Total Warfare presents a set of generic conventional infantry
and difficult to maintain. In addition, the process of training                             units, as noted on the Generic Conventional Infantry Units Table
MechWarriors and vehicle crews is time-consuming and costly.                                (see p. 213). The table also notes Movement Points and the
By comparison, infantry can be recruited and given rudimen-                                 number of men in a full-strength unit of each type. The basic
tary training and equipment rapidly and inexpensively. There                                units under each infantry type—a Rifle (Ballistic) Foot Platoon,
is almost no limit to the number of infantry troops that can be                             Machine Gun (Ballistic, Support) Motorized Platoon and so
thrown, willing or unwilling, into battle.                                                  on—can represent different kinds of weapons, though they all
   Though many stellar empires regularly include infantry as                                fall under the general description of the unit type in question.
part of integrated ’Mech and vehicle forces, the strength of                                For example, a Rifle (Ballistic) Platoon can field standard rifles,
infantry reveals itself most in defensive operations, which                                 Gauss rifles, gyrojet rifles, needlers and so on (or any combina-
bypass the difficulty of transporting and feeding a mass                                    tion thereof ).
interstellar army. Infantry forces also rarely last long against                               Finally, it should be noted that the specific number of troop-
BattleMechs, being heavily outgunned by those behemoths of                                  ers per platoon are for game purposes, regardless of how the
the battlefield. Even in offensive operations, however, infan-                              sourcebook or story fiction has previously described the number
try can sometimes inflict enough damage to turn the tide of                                 of troopers per a given platoon type.
battle, and can become a serious threat in confined areas such                                 Record Sheets: As noted under Record Sheets, p. 11, blank,
as dense woods and urban zones.                                                             generic record sheets for conventional infantry are found in

readout. While such differences rarely change the basic game                                   Though conventional infantry using the infantry platoon

LSLS                                                                                              dures and so on (see the Combat section, p. 98).

                                                                                                   Conventional Infantry Attacks
                                                                                                     While conventional infantry use the same range modifiers
                                                                                                   as ’Mechs and vehicles, unlike those units, infantry can attack
                                                                                                   another unit in the same hex. The Conventional Infantry Range
                                                                                                   Modifier Table (see p. 215) shows the to-hit modifiers for each
                                                                                                   platoon type at 0 range; for convenience, the table also lists the
                                                                                                   range to-hit modifiers for every generic platoon type, out to their
                                                                                                   maximum range in hexes. Because conventional infantry units
                                                                                                   only have a single weapon attack, they can only target a single
                                                                                                   unit for a weapon attack during a given Weapon Attack Phase.

                                                                          SWARM ATTACKS
      ANTI-’MECH ATTACKS                                                     Infantry units that begin a Weapon Attack Phase in the same
                                                                          hex and at the same elevation(s) of an enemy ’Mech may choose
    Most infantry units can make specialized anti-’Mech attacks,          to swarm the ’Mech, rather than use their weapons or attack its
 which involve closing with a ’Mech, climbing it and planting             legs. Swarm attacks represent the boldest and most dangerous
 satchel charges in strategic, vulnerable locations. However, for such    attacks that infantry can perform against a ’Mech. A unit making a
 attacks to have any real hope of success, an infantry platoon must       normal swarm attack rushes a ’Mech, grapples and climbs it, and
 be composed of dedicated, highly skilled troops that have under-         then inflicts damage against the MechWarrior or the upper parts
 gone significant training. Despite its near-suicidal aspect, this type   of the ’Mech in the next turn. A unit can only be swarmed by one
 of attack successfully executed can quickly turn the tide of battle.     infantry unit, and only one swarm attack can be made against a
    Resolve anti-’Mech attacks in the Weapon Attack Phase of the turn.    target in a given turn.
    Body-Mounted Missile Launchers: Inner Sphere battle armor                The base to-hit number is the attacking unit’s Anti-’Mech Skill
 units with body-mounted missile launchers cannot make anti-              Rating, modified by the number of troopers currently active in
’Mech attacks until they jettison such launchers (the controlling         the unit (as shown on the Swarm Attacks Table). The more troop-
 player can announce he is ejecting the missile launchers at any          ers, the greater the chance of success. Modify the infantry unit’s
 time, marking them off on the appropriate record sheet).                 base to-hit number as normal for target movement and terrain,
    Anti-´Mech Skill: Different types of infantry have different          and if the ’Mech is prone or immobile.
 base Anti-’Mech Skills, and so players should review the Average            The swarm attack to-hit roll determines only if the infantry unit
 Skills Table, p. 40, before making this kind of attack.                  manages to gain secure footholds on the ’Mech. The infantry unit
    Prohibited Units: Mechanized infantry cannot make anti-               does not inflict damage on the ’Mech during either the Weapon
’Mech attacks.                                                            or Physical attack phases of this turn.
    IndustrialMechs: Against IndustrialMechs, anti-´Mech attacks             A swarming unit cannot make attacks against any other target
 receive a –1 to-hit modifier.                                            other than the unit it is swarming, or mechanized battle armor
    ProtoMechs: ProtoMechs cannot be targeted by anti-´Mech               being carried by the unit it is swarming. In turn, a unit perform-
 attacks.                                                                 ing a swarming attack may not be the target of weapon attacks.
    TAG: An infantry unit may not designate a target and make an          A swarming unit may end a swarming attack during any subse-
 Anti-’Mech Attack in the same turn.                                      quent Weapon Attack Phase. It is then placed in the hex contain-
    Vehicles: Vehicles can be the target of a swarm attack, a type        ing the target ’Mech with no further effects.
 of anti-´Mech attack (see Swarm Attacks, at right).                         Aimed Shots: As with leg attacks, if the target unit is immobile,

     A Dark Caste Bombardier searches for the last remaining survivors of a decisive raid.

 Hexes 8, 9 and 10 are prohibited because entering them would
 require a level change.
    If the King Karnov was destroyed upon landing in the hex, the
 controlling player would need to redistribute the infantry units at
 the end of the Movement Phase, in order to keep from violating the
 stacking rules. Hex 6 requires the least number of MP to enter, and                                       • infantry dismounting diagram •
 so the controlling player moves two infantry units there first. He then
 moves two more infantry units into Hex 7, and he can leave two more
 infantry units in Hex C. This leaves him with two infantry units he
 cannot redistribute, either because they are prohibited from entering                             MECHANIZED BATTLE
 the hex or they would violate the stacking rules. These two units are
 destroyed, leaving him with six Motorized Infantry Platoons to move                                    ARMOR
 next turn.
